sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Ruchel.
When she came back, the German were already in Rovno.
And she was wounded with a shrapnel in her arm.
She was in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE].
She was in the Jewish hospital.
And a friend of ours, a neighbor, she went to visit her to the hospital.
And Ruchel was constantly crying.
Where's the mama?
Where's the dada?
She thought that we'd got killed by the bombs.
Nobody.
No.
They-- they all told that we got killed by the bombs.
And my-- my mother's brothers-- she had four brothers and wives and children-- and my father's-- Nobody know where.
No.
Nobody.
No.
We just left.
We didn't have a chance to talk to them.
We didn't have a chance.
So when Ruchel came back, what I start to tell you, she was wounded and she was in the [INAUDIBLE] and that neighbor of ours went to visit her a few times.
So she was constantly crying.
She didn't know what has happened to the mama, to the dada, to us.
So-- and when the first [INAUDIBLE] begin-- that was in-- in 1941, November 6 and 7, they took out first the sick people from the hospitals, and the old people, and they went first.
They were first killed under the-- it is like a suburb of Rovno.
It was a big forest.
It's called the [?
Sosnokis, ?] and that's what-- that's what she got killed there.
With other 18,500 people for the two days.
That's what the neighbor advised when we came back.
That I'll come [? when ?] we came back from where we were.
When she saw, she said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH].
That's what we got.
Yes.
She's-- she knows about.
She told us.
Yeah.
We traveled-- we started by walking.
But then when the night broke and was a lot of people, so the-- the Russian army was backing deeper to Russia.
And they took us off one of those big trucks.
And we were traveling a whole night, a whole day.
That was already Thursday, the 26th.
And we came to Kiev.
In Kiev, they put us in a place for-- school, in a school place-- but all the [INAUDIBLE] were there.
No.
But when we came to Kiev, I saw a few people there, a few neighbors, you know.
Two boys, like my girlfriend's two brothers, and another-- he was a photographer.
He was also there.
So we saw a lot of people there.
But everybody was looking for themself because we couldn't stay too long.
The German was going, like, you know, town after town.
So we couldn't stay in Kiev too long.
So we went from Kiev, we went to Kharkov.
From Kharkov we went to Dnipropetrovsk, and from Dnipropetrovsk, we went to Krasnodarsky Krai.
And we stopped in a town, Rostov.
Rostov [INAUDIBLE].
This is a big town and it has this name after the-- the river, Don.
So we would stop there.
When you were preparing to leave your home,  what did you take with you?
Nothing.
Nothing.
The way we were.
Nothing.

We couldn't take nothing with us, because we were just this one more incident that across the street from us was a big building, like a three story building-- a new building-- and we lived across the street.
But no-- no matter what, you know, we used to go across the street and hide in that building and wait until til-- [INAUDIBLE], and then we-- we out.
But this time it was-- it was June 25th.
My mother cooked something, which she prepared on the table.
We just sit to eat about something.
An alert, I mean, the bombs.
So what we do, we leave-- left everything and we're running across to that place to hide.
In the meantime, my mo-- my mother was running, she lost her slipper.
So she didn't go-- she went without-- without a slipper.
And-- and the back of that building, it was like a lumber yard-- a lot of lumber.
So my father was running.
My sister, my mother, and I was after them.
But we didn't go into that building.
He said, let's go to the-- we'll hide under the [NON-ENGLISH], under the-- the lumber.
All right.
So we waited.
It maybe took about 20 minutes.
And when walked out from that place, the roof was on the floor.
Everybody got killed in that place, that we were always going there.
We heard a lot of screaming and a lot of wounded were running out with bloody people.
And that's all.
My father said, we're not staying.
We're going.
We're not-- we're going to-- everybody's going to get killed here.
And that's with my father, my mother, my sister, and myself.
So that like I start to tell you, we went to-- we were already in Krasnodarskiy Krai.
Yeah.
That's what we survived there.
So it took us about three weeks from Rostov to go-- my father wants to go-- to Georgia.
There, over there, is a war-- a warm climate.
No winter there.
We didn't have nothing with us.
No-- only what we were wearing.
But when we stopped, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like I said, it was about three weeks.
And going down this way, they-- they stopped us in [?
Baku. ?] [?
Baku, ?] they have all the-- the-- the [? niev, ?] you know, the oil.
Yeah.
And they tell us to go-- to go.
You can't go further because they need all the-- these trains they need for the-- to send to the front with the-- with the-- with the--
With the soldiers.
Right.
Right.
So we stopped there.
It wasn't even there.
We'd stopped at another station.
It's called Derbent.
And over there, we stopped.
They took us off from the train and said, you can't go farther.
This is it.
So we were waiting on the-- on this train station.
And, you know, the-- the night was approaching.
It was summer.
It was still warm.
So my father said, you sit here.
I'll go.
I'll see the town.
I'll see how it looks.
All right.
He went.
A few hours, my father came back.
He was tol-- telling us about the-- the town.
It's-- it's Derbent.
It's the-- on-- on one side, is the Caucasun Mountains, and on the other side is the Caspian-- Caspian Sea.
And this was the whole town.
Yeah.
So, he said, he met up-- my father was very, you know, he was talking to anybody.
So he met a couple-- Jewish people-- and said, he was telling them [NON-ENGLISH] And they said, bring them.
They're going to stay with us a night or two till you find something.
All right.
So we went to that-- to that place.
They had a beautiful home, everything.
But we were just sleeping wherever it was possible.
You know, after a few days, we find sort of a place where to stay.
Probably we have.
We have Russian money, you know.
Money.
Money we have.
We have money.
Yeah.
So we find a-- an-- an apartment.
It was a one room.
One room.
No floor.
Nothing.
Nothing in the room.
Just, like, you know, on the floor.
So but in order to be there, you have to have a job.
Otherwise, if you don't have a job, they don't give you rationed bread.
You have to have 500 gram bread, but you have to work.
So my father was a painter.
Right away, he went all over and he was looking for jobs.
But all the institutions, like the schools and the theaters, everything was converted for hospitals.
They used to bring the first wounded to Dar-- Derbent.
And they had a lot of hospitals and doctors.
So my father got the job as a painter.
He'd have to refresh the-- fresh up the-- the rooms.
My mother helped him.
So they had already a job, and they had the 500 gram bread.
I know how to sew, so I went in and I was, you know-- went into a [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.
They used to sew for the-- for the public, but then they need everything for the army.
So they used to make underwear.
[NON-ENGLISH] and that was went to the-- everything for the army.
So I had a job also, and I had my 500 gram bread.
My sister, Pesie, she was only 13 years old.
She was going to school.
She was wanted to play the piano, but forget about it.
And she was working in a factory that were making, like, food, like, [NON-ENGLISH] sell them.
You know, whatever [INAUDIBLE], meat, you know, fish.
She-- she had a job there, so everybody has a  job.
So we started it.
But beside this, we didn't have nothing.
We have to-- have to have something to wear, and it was so-- everything was expensive.
And because this used to be very warm there.
But, just like they say, they said, the [?
Berzenikas, ?] they brought the cold weather.
They was blaming us.
It was bitter cold.
Not so cold but rainy-- rainy weather.
Sluggish, you know.
Rainy.
Yeah.
So.
But the first two winters was terrible.
We didn't have what to eat.
Only for the 500 gram bread we have to.
And you want to buy something on the bazaar, it was-- everything was expensive.
And they give you the bread, you know-- today they give you the bread for tomorrow, and my mother used to hide it.
No.
No.
The only thing we kept is Yom Kippur.
We had, you know, there was-- there was Jewish people-- Russian Jewish, a lot.
They know.
Yeah.
We didn't-- we didn't know.
Well, we didn't work on Shabbas.
Yeah, that day-- this was closed.
But no.
No.
We stayed there from 1941 till 1945.
We stayed there for four years.
Well, the day-- you know, as the years goes, we'd have our-- our [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, every-- the Polish people, they organized like a little-- a little club.
I used to go there and listen to speakers and-- and know what's going on in the world.
Yeah, we know.
Yeah.
And then, when the [INAUDIBLE] army, they-- they started it, they want to help with the [NON-ENGLISH].
So they helped us.
They used to get a little rationing, like flour or some things.
So that helped.
Sugar.
Otherwise, we were really, really bad-- in bad shape.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Not-- not when we came to Rovno.
When we came back to Rovno, we didn't know.
We didn't-- we didn't have an idea about concentration camps.
We didn't have no idea what was going on.
But we know that something, you know, that Hitler kills.
But we didn't know more specific, what-- what about concentration camps.
We didn't know.
No.
Well, we last saw Rovno, Rovno was burning.
Yeah.
So that depiction remained in my-- in my-- in my memory.
Rovno [NON-ENGLISH], right?
But then, when the war ended, that was in 1944? 1944, I think?
I don't know-- I'm not sure, but we did-- we couldn't go out to Rovno right away.
It took us another almost a year.
But was still, the [INAUDIBLE] with all those-- those-- [INAUDIBLE] it was still active.
So-- and so, the thing is this.
My father took two-- two wounded people.
They were already-- the war was over.
And he said, I'm going to bring them to home.
They need somebody to, like, chaperone.
So my father took the one.
They were going to Ukraine, like to-- to Odessa or to Kharkov.
So my father said, I'm going to take.
And from there, I want to go to Rovno.
So he left us in Derbent, and he took those two people.
He pla-- took them to their villages, and from there he went to Rovno.
He went to Rovno, so he saw what's-- what's-- what's doing.
And he went into, like you say, like, the-- the city hall, and he said, I'm from Rovno.
My family's there.
I need papers that we are Rovno.
Otherwise they wouldn't let us out.
So he went to Rovno.
He brought us those papers, those important papers.
He came back to Derbent, and he took us back to Rovno.
What did you see when you returned?
Well, we saw, when we stopped by train, we saw-- we saw all Rovno, because no buildings.
You can see all Rovno.
No-- no buildings.
No nothing that was left.
Yeah.
So--
Later on.
Later on, yeah.
We saw former neighbors.
That neighbor, that she told us about my sister, her name was Munya Danziker.
She told us about my sister when we came back.
Yeah.
And then, as the time goes, you know, a lot of people came back from Russia.
And, you know, like, they were in the army or whatever.
And my father used to go on the-- on the bazaar and he used to bring people to our house.
And my mother was cooking for them and making them [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
We still-- we still didn't have an idea about the-- what-- about concentration camp.
We still-- we still didn't know.
We still didn't know.
When we came to Poland, after we left Rovno, then-- then we know.
Yeah.
No.
Not from my time.
I was born, the Poles-- Polish was already independence country.
They gave Pole-- no.
They didn't have any.
It was incident, you know.
Like, you know, always anti-Semitic [INAUDIBLE].
Well, they were not in Rovno.
Not so much.
Rovno had our [NON-ENGLISH] that they were playing-- they were playing soccer.
They were the best [NON-ENGLISH].
So when they won, they-- they were jealous.
You know, they were jealous.
So they used to-- once, [INAUDIBLE], we had very strong people, and they-- they were afraid.
Yeah.
Not-- not very serious, you know.
But you heard that there is-- that the Poles used to say, don't-- don't buy-- don't buy by the Jews.
[INAUDIBLE].
You know.
This is all the-- I mean, [INAUDIBLE].
Iran.
So that was-- that was-- yeah.
But nothing-- nothing serious.
In the bigger town, like in Warsaw and Krakow, there were more incidents, because there were universities there.
They were-- they were more serious.
But not in Rovno.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That, as a matter of fact, one neighbor recognized my father.
And he said that he saw my mother's brother, and he was in the ghetto.
A year after they destroyed the 18,500, they left a few people-- the strong ones.
My mother's brother was a strong man-- a tall, strong man.
So he was telling my mother thought he saw Lebl.
He looked very skinny.
That-- that's all.
Yeah.
My father lost three brothers and a sister.
They all have children.
My mother lost four brothers, children.
And I lost my sister.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Nazis.
Seven months, yeah.
In the meantime, my husband returned back.
He was-- he was also-- he was in Russia also.
He came back.
Yeah.
No.
When my husband came back, he had cousins there.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH].
They said, come out.
There's a family here.
There's two girls and they have friends and a mother and a father.
So my husband and his cousin came to see us.
It was, you know-- it was sort of nice.
My husband's name?
Zindel.
Yeah.
So when he came in and he saw what's going on, he didn't want to go out.
So in a month later, we were married.
My husband was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was, like you could say, like a wonder child, you know.
The-- the-- the choir leaders used to come to the shivas and pick up the little boys that they have good voices.
He start to sing when he was about 12 years old.
And he was singing with the greatest [NON-ENGLISH] with [?
Elshman, ?] with [?
Zarota. ?] He was-- yeah.
So that really, when the war broke out, he also-- he escaped.
Don't know other way.
But this saved him.
He was, like, in a place that they go entertain the troops, like you have here Bob Hope.
That help-- that saved him.
He was constantly working, you know, travelling around, and entertaining the troops.
And that saved him.
But he wants to come back.
So he came back, and he came out to us.
And he was a very handsome man.
And a month later we were married.
My husband had a few cousins.
All-- everybody survived.
They all-- all from Russia.
I didn't have no family, just except my-- my-- my mother, my father.
A distant cousin came also, you know, my father's-- from my father's side.
We had a nice wedding.
My mother was preparing the whole wedding.
We have a regular wedding with a rabbi, [INAUDIBLE] Rovno, [INAUDIBLE].
He gave us [NON-ENGLISH] Kiddushin.
I still have the [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
I have.
And the chuppah was outside.
Yeah.
We remain about another month.
We were, all in all, we were about seven months.
Then we-- we said, we decided we want to go to the free world.
We don't want to stay in Russia.
We want to go.
So in order to go, you have to go first to Poland.
So we came with my mother, my father, my sister, and my husband.
We went to a town, Bytom.
Then we went to Zabrze.
In Zabrze, was kibbutz already, you know, for all  the survivors from all our people.
They form a kibbutz.
And they took us into the-- to that kibbutz, and I start traveling with them, you know.
So you didn't stay in one place too long, because everything was illegal going to Israel.
It just illegal.
But in order to, you have to still work in the-- be in the kibbutz.
So first stop was in Czechoslovakia.
There we celebrated Pesach, the first time after the war we celebrated.
We have a big Seder.
Yeah.
When were you able to observe the Jewish rituals after the war?
What was the first point that you felt free to--
When we came to-- when we stopped in Austria.
That was-- that was already-- that was already my home.
Yeah.
So we went there, and then from there we went to-- we were climbing the Alps, and it was very, very [INAUDIBLE], because everything was illegal.
You're not supposed to.
Everything, you know.
The guard make believe they don't see.
Everything was fixed.
But in the meantime you have to be alert.
Yeah.
So finally, we stopped in Austria.
We went to Vienna, and they have a big place, it's called [INAUDIBLE].
Everybody was [INAUDIBLE] at [INAUDIBLE].
But you couldn't stay too long, because other people.
It was like a transit-- a transit place.
So from there they took us to a smaller town, Linz.
Over there in Linz was a big DP camp.
It was about 2,000 people.
In most, the people-- there were Holocaust survivors, mostly from [?
Katzett. ?] So my husband has a friend there.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He get-- he got us-- it was like an apartment.
Three rooms that we share.
Three families shared the room with a kitchen.
And we-- we stayed there.
Yeah.
It wasn't so bad.
We were-- this, it was the American zone.
We  were in the [?
Amerikanerzone. ?] Yeah.
So we got, you know-- they gave us-- so they-- they supported us by the UNRRA, by the Joint, and by the HAIS.
So we had what to eat. you know, they gave us, you know, like margarine and bread and all those-- all those things.
But it was-- it was OK.
When did you first realize what happened during the war?
At what point did you get this information?
When we came to the-- when we came to that [NON-ENGLISH].
We saw the people and they were telling us horror stories while they-- while they-- in the kibbutz even, they had a few-- two girls, two sisters.
And they were telling us how the way they select them with everything.
It was-- so our story, being in Russia, wasn't-- you know?
Yeah.
That's what we realize.
Yeah.
Well, first, in 19-- Europe I left in 1949.
But in the meantime, they formed-- everything wasn't-- nothing there established, so my husband and another friend, they made a schul.
When my husband came, they made a schul.
And they were davening, my husband had a choir, and it was beginning to-- to look like a little Yiddish.
Yeah.
That was a mikvah.
Yeah.
You had a mikvah.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
There was-- there's a lot of Hungarian people.
The Hungarian-- Hungarian, Romanian, they--
No.
They have to go to-- to Linz.
They have to go to Linz.
But it wasn't-- wasn't, you know.
You have money, you buy.
You have money, you buy.
Yeah.
In the meantime, in 1947 my eldest son was born.
Norman.
Nucham. ?] We call him [?
Nucham. ?] Norman.
When we came to America, they said, what kind of name is [?
Nucham? ?] So I said, well, how should I call him?
All right.
So Norman was born in March 6, 1947.
I came to America-- well, this is the time now.
We came right after Thanksgiving in 1949.
Yeah.
We were with-- on a boat for about 11 days.
Everybody was sick.
Sick, sick, sick.
I-- I was one of them.
[LAUGHS]
Couldn't eat.
Couldn't look at food.
But I lived on-- on grapefruit.
But I didn't know about grapefruit like-- like orange, you know.
It was good food, but I couldn't-- who could eat?
Yeah.
Everybody was sick.
Did you have other children?
I went-- yeah. 19-- 1953, my youngest son was born, Henry.
He was born in America.
Well, my husband-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH], like I told you.
He was a [NON-ENGLISH].
He got right away got a position.
Yeah.
His first position was on Vermont Street.
That was in east New York.
And they paid him pretty good.
Yeah.
And from then on, he was more well known, everything.
Yeah.
But I want to tell you about my first apartment when we came to America.
It was in Brownsville, on 27 Bristol Street.
We lived in Brownsville for eight years.
Yeah.
It was hard.
Nobody supported us.
Nobody gave us anything.
No.
I could not work, because I have to take care of the first, the child, then the children.
I could not work.
No.
Oh, now?
Oh.
Now I'm-- I call myself, like, a society lady.
[LAUGHS] Not exactly.
Well, I'm a volunteer, first of all, in this building.
In this building, I live 14 years.
And I think-- that's the best thing that could happen with me.
You see?
It's OK.
I have a lot of friends.
I'm active.
I'm the-- the treasurer of the tenants association.
And I take care on a lot of things.
And this really keeps me going.
I'm always busy.
When they need somebody, they have a Ru-- a new Russian comes in, they need a translator, I come.
I help.
And when the first Russians start to come, I worked for the-- for the Jewish Congress.
I was also a translator.
I like it.
I speak the language perfect.
So for me, it's no problem.
But we came to America, I didn't know a word English.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Well, it's-- it's already 47 years.
It's-- yeah.
Yes.
I'm the recording secretary for my society, from the Rovno Society.
It's already four years that I'm-- I'm doing all the reports.
I'm writing.
And this has to be done in Yiddish.
So that's my-- I love it a lot, you know.
I come and I give the reports.
And, you know-- and the people that with me, you know, those are my-- my friends.
Just a few left, but we-- we're very, very close.
Those are my [INAUDIBLE].
Probably, we have meetings, about six or seven meetings a year.
But we have executive meetings in between.
So I have to be there.
Oh, yeah.
I was-- I was, you know, first of all, my children, my grandchildren, they got to-- to fled [NON-ENGLISH].
So I was invited two times, especially my-- my grandson.
Grandma, you have to come.
You have to tell the story.
So I was there twice.
So the children listen to me and ask me question.
I really like it.
And last-- this past July, I was invited to Columbia University to speak to a group of people-- young student-- that are interested in Yiddish.
So I wrote my own lecture and I was talking to them.
And this was really-- for me, it was a-- what an experience.
I didn't know what, you know, what's what.
But soon as I saw the people, young people, in their '20s, I said, OK.
No problem.
We'll do fine.
Yeah.
And then, I go with my-- with my youngest son.
He has a [INAUDIBLE].
We're going there for the-- for the 22nd of November till the-- 22nd till the 27th.
And, there's everything in Yiddish there.
Yeah.
So I'm also-- I'll teach them how to cook and how to-- sometimes I sing for them some things I'll-- It's-- it works out good.
I do my time good.
